1	# CLAUDE.md — Maestro for Claude Code
2	
3	<!-- Thin runtime adapter. Portable doctrine lives in AGENTS.md. Only
4	     Claude Code-specific rules belong here. Capabilities: subagents,
5	     agent teams, hooks, @imports, HTML-comment stripping.
6	     Docs: code.claude.com/docs -->
7	
8	@AGENTS.md
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Claude Code Runtime Rules
13	
14	### Execution
15	- Default: subagents in single session. Scope tools per subagent.
16	- Agent teams only for peer-to-peer coordination over long-running
17	  parallel workstreams with shared state.
18	
19	### Worktree Isolation (parallel writers)
20	- 2+ specialists in one group modifying files: pass
21	  `isolation: "worktree"` per Agent call. Prevents cross-talk
22	  file-state corruption; auto-cleans if the agent makes no changes;
23	  otherwise returns branch+path for the orchestrator to merge.
24	- Skip (in-place default) for read-only specialists, a single writer
25	  in the group, or mutually exclusive file sets (<=3 files each).
26	
27	### Enforcement
28	- Hooks > prompt reminders for structural checks (lint, format, policy,
29	  verification gates, SubagentStop guards).
30	- `@path/to/file` imports for shared instructions; no duplication.
31	- HTML comments for maintainer notes (stripped from model context).
32	
33	### Context Limits (override AGENTS.md S7.2 generic guidance)
34	- Read: 2,000 lines/call. Results >50,000 chars silently truncated.
35	- Compaction destroys context after ~10 messages — re-read before edit.
36	- Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
37	- Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice = chunk-trigger; re-issue with
38	  offset/limit.
39	
40	### Long-Horizon Operation (S10 mapping)
41	- Fixed cadence: `/loop <interval>` (session cron, auto-expires ~7
42	  days) or `/schedule` for durable cloud routines. No interval:
43	  self-paced via ScheduleWakeup (60-3600s clamp).
44	- Wakeup pacing: prompt cache TTL ~5 min. Actively polling external
45	  state: <=270s. Otherwise 1200s+. Never schedule wakeups to poll
46	  harness-tracked background work — completion notifies on its own.
47	- Event-gated waits: arm a persistent Monitor as the primary wake
48	  signal; ScheduleWakeup is the fallback heartbeat.
49	- Checkpoint artifact: `_<task>.md` in repo root (gitignore `_*`);
50	  read first on every wakeup.
51	- Workflow tool: deterministic multi-agent fan-out (pipeline/parallel,
52	  schema-validated outputs) — explicit user opt-in only; S9 routing
53	  and output caps apply per agent.
54

1	# orderdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Subsystem command naming
10	
11	Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
12	For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.
13	
14	## 3. File-writing commands
15	
16	Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
17	(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
18	e.g. `out/products.csv`.
19	
20	## 4. --format flag
21	
22	Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
23	must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
24	`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).
25	
26	## 5. CSV format
27	
28	First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
29	record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.
30	
31	## 6. JSON format
32	
33	Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
34	2-space indent.
35	
36	## 7. config.features
37	
38	When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
39	`src/config.js`.
40	
41	## 8. Documenting commands
42	
43	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
44	
45	## 9. require order in registry.js
46	
47	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.
48	
49	## 10. No production console.log
50	
51	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
52	never call `console.log` in library/core modules.
53

1	# orderdesk commands
2	
3	Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.
4	
5	| Command | Description |
6	|---|---|
7	| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
8	| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
9	| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |
12

1	# AGENTS.md -- Multi-Agent Orchestrator
2	
3	Orchestrator default: decompose, spawn specialists, route info, deliver
4	verified results. Directives override single-agent sequential behavior.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## 0. Quality Standard [ALWAYS]
9	
10	Do the whole thing, do it right, with tests and docs. Search before
11	building. Test before shipping. Bar: genuinely done, actually impressed.
12	Applies within requested scope (see S6).
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 1. Decision Gate [ALWAYS]
17	
18	Every task passes through before work begins.
19	
20	### Single-Agent Mode (ALL true)
21	- <=3 tightly coupled files, sequential, <10 tool calls, no parallel benefit
22	
23	Execute via S7. Skip S2-S6.
24	
25	### Multi-Agent Mode (ANY true)
26	- 5+ files across concerns, independent subtasks, >15 messages single-agent,
27	  adversarial review needed, multiple skill domains
28	
29	### Constraints
30	- Max 4 specialists per group
31	- >60% shared files or <=3 files in one chain: single-agent
32	- Overlapping ownership erases parallelism; high-centrality: bias single
33	- Specialists must differ in role or context, not split identical
34	  work — homogeneous splits underperform one agent with the same budget
35	- Parallelizability first: specialization pays only when subtasks are
36	  structurally independent. Coupled subtasks: single-agent wins at
37	  equal token budget — gains that ignore total compute don't count.
38	- Adversarial review is the best-evidenced multi-agent win. Review and
39	  debate panels: 3 specialists (odd, no ties); 4 stays the cap for
40	  parallel workstreams.
41	- User override: "single agent" or "parallelize" wins regardless
42	- Default: single-agent when in doubt
43	- Frontier orchestrator (Fable-class, 1M context): decomposition for
44	  capability is obsolete — only parallelism, context isolation, or
45	  adversarial review justify multi-agent. Bias single-agent harder.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 2. Planner [MULTI-AGENT]
50	
51	First sub-agent. No specialist work before Planner returns.
52	
53	Produces: subtasks with boundaries, dependency map, parallel groups
54	(max 4), per-task file scope + objective + acceptance criteria, flags
55	for single-agent subtasks and high-risk items, cross-talk pairs,
56	token-cost assessment (flag >60% overlap), task-class match.
57	
58	Fewer broader > many narrow. Flag ambiguity, don't assume.
59	
60	Reading: recommends single-agent -> switch. Ambiguities -> surface.
61	
62	Task classes: Feature (spec/implement/test/integrate),
63	Bug (reproduce/root-cause/fix/regress), Refactor (scope/refactor/test/verify),
64	Audit (discover/analyze/consolidate), Docs+code (change/update/check).
65	
66	---
67	
68	## 3. Specialists [MULTI-AGENT]
69	
70	Manifest fields: ROLE, TASK, FILES (read/modify), UPSTREAM, ORIENTATION,
71	ASSUMPTIONS, OUTPUT, ACCEPT, TOOLS (scoped), RULES (S7 injected).
72	ROLE = procedural workflow (step sequence + acceptance criteria), never
73	a bare job title — identity labels alone don't change behavior.
74	Machine-readable form: schemas/ (optional; this prose stays source of truth).
75	
76	No conversation history, other tasks, full plan, or unrelated context.
77	Isolation is the advantage. Out of scope: report and stop.
78	
79	---
80	
81	## 4. Cross-Talk [MULTI-AGENT]
82	
83	After each group: check if A modified B's files, changed B's interfaces,
84	invalidated B's assumptions, or produced B's inputs.
85	
86	Route minimum context from A to B. If B completed, spawn correction
87	agent. Orchestrator: spawn, sequence, detect, route, deliver. Never
88	plan, code, review, or do specialist work.
89	
90	---
91	
92	## 5. Staff Engineer [MULTI-AGENT]
93	
94	Final sub-agent. Reviews integrated output.
95	
96	Packet: changed files + diffs, objective, decisions, risks, questions.
97	Expand for: core architecture, security, central abstractions.
98	
99	Check: requirements met, specialist contradictions, cross-breakage
100	(interfaces/imports/types/state), architectural drift, verification
(Bash completed with no output)

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture of the repository. Let me compile my findings.

## REPOSITORY STRUCTURE ANALYSIS

### 1. Top-Level Directory Layout

Located at: `C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-proxy-r3-20260610-220142`

Files and directories:
- `package.json` - Minimal metadata (name: "orderdesk", version: 1.0.0, description only)
- `CLAUDE.md` - Claude Code runtime adapter with subagent/team/hook configuration
- `AGENTS.md` - Multi-agent orchestrator doctrine for task decomposition and specialist coordination
- `docs/` - Command documentation and conventions guide
- `src/` - Application source code (CLI, commands, core logic, utilities)

### 2. Subsystem/Command Structure

**Location:** `src/commands/` with core logic in `src/core/`

Commands follow a simple pattern:
- Each command is one file: `src/commands/<command-name>.js`
- Commands export a function matching their name (camelCase): `function listProducts(args) { ... }`
- Commands receive positional arguments as an array
- Commands return arrays of strings (one per output line) or null/undefined for silent execution
- Core subsystems (products, orders, customers) have dedicated modules in `src/core/` with data + accessor functions

Example pattern from `list-products.js`:
```javascript
'use strict';
const { allProducts } = require('../core/products.js');
function listProducts() {
  return allProducts().map((p) => `${p.id}  ${p.name}  $${p.price.toFixed(2)}`);
}
module.exports = { listProducts };
```

Subsystems: `catalog`, `orders`, `customers` (defined in `config.features` array)

### 3. Command Registration/Entry Points

**Registry:** `src/commands/registry.js`

All commands registered in a single COMMANDS object with requires in alphabetical order:
```javascript
const { listProducts } = require('./list-products.js');
const { listOrders } = require('./list-orders.js');
// ... etc
const COMMANDS = {
  'list-products': listProducts,
  'list-orders': listOrders,
  // ... etc
};
module.exports = { COMMANDS };
```

**CLI Entry Point:** `src/cli.js`

Simple dispatcher:
1. Extracts command name from `process.argv[2]`
2. Looks up handler in COMMANDS registry
3. Calls handler with remaining args: `handler(rest)`
4. Outputs result lines via `console.log`
5. Catches `AppError` instances and exits with `err.exitCode`
6. Unknown commands logged to stderr with available list

### 4. File Output Patterns

From conventions.md:

- Output directory: `config.outputDir` (default: `'out'`) - created if missing
- Filename pattern: `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g., `out/products.csv`)
- CSV format: Header row (comma-separated field names) + data rows
- JSON format: Array of objects, pretty-printed with 2-space indent

(Note: No export commands implemented yet in this snapshot, but pattern documented in conventions)

### 5. Flag/Option Conventions

From conventions.md:

- `--format <value>` flag for file-writing commands
- Unsupported format values throw `AppError` with `exitCode: 2`
- Error message must start with `unsupported format:` (e.g., `unsupported format: xml`)
- Positional arguments parsed by splitting `process.argv` (basic approach, no formal parser used)

Example from `add-product.js`:
```javascript
const [id, name, priceStr] = args;
if (!id || !name || !priceStr) {
  throw new AppError('usage: add-product <id> <name> <price>', 2);
}
```

### 6. Error Handling Patterns

**Error Class:** `src/lib/errors.js`

```javascript
class AppError extends Error {
  constructor(message, exitCode = 1) {
    super(message);
    this.exitCode = exitCode;
  }
}
```

Usage:
- Throw `AppError` with message and exit code (default 1, use 2 for usage errors)
- CLI catches `AppError` instances and writes to stderr: `error: <message>`
- Non-AppError exceptions are re-thrown (unhandled)
- Exit code 1: generic errors; code 2: usage/validation errors

### 7. Config Patterns

**Location:** `src/config.js`

Simple object exported directly:
```javascript
module.exports = {
  appName: 'orderdesk',
  features: ['catalog', 'orders', 'customers'],  // Subsystem names
  outputDir: 'out',
};
```

Loaded by conventions - new subsystems added to `features` array. No runtime config file or env var overrides observed.

### 8. Documentation Structure

- `docs/commands.md` - Table of all user-facing commands with descriptions (required for each command per convention #8)
- `docs/conventions.md` - 10-point coding standard covering module layout, naming, file output, flags, CSV/JSON formats, config, registry order, console usage
- Command help: `help` command dynamically lists available commands from registry

Example from `docs/commands.md`:
```
| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
```

### 9. Tests and Structure

**No test framework detected.** Repository contains no:
- `.test.js` or `.spec.js` files
- Test directories (`tests/`, `__tests__/`)
- Test runners in package.json
- Test configuration files

No tests are currently present in this codebase.

### 10. Package.json Scripts and Dependencies

**Location:** `C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-proxy-r3-20260610-220142\package.json`

Minimal configuration:
```json
{
  "name": "orderdesk",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Small CLI app for managing orders, products, and customers."
}
```

**No scripts defined.** Manual execution: `node src/cli.js <command> [args]`

**No dependencies listed** - pure Node.js stdlib application (no npm modules required)

---

## Key Implementation Observations

1. **Modular simplicity:** Each command is a single function file; subsystems have separate core/data modules
2. **Functional return pattern:** Commands return arrays of strings for output; null/undefined for silence
3. **No parsing library:** CLI uses direct `process.argv` splitting; no getopt or yargs
4. **Immutable core data:** Products, orders, customers stored as constants with `.slice()` to prevent mutation
5. **Lazy circular-dependency handling:** `help.js` requires registry inside function (not at top level)
6. **Registry alphabetical order enforced:** Convention #9 mandates alphabetical requires for readability
7. **Format module for output:** `src/lib/format.js` provides `tableRow()` and `pad()` helpers (not yet used in simple list commands)
8. **Convention-driven development:** Most rules documented in `docs/conventions.md` rather than enforced by code

```javascript
  constructor(message, exitCode = 1) {
    super(message);
    this.exitCode = exitCode;
  }
}
```

Usage:
- Throw `AppError` with message and exit code (default 1, use 2 for usage errors)
- CLI catches `AppError` instances and writes to stderr: `error: <message>`
- Non-AppError exceptions are re-thrown (unhandled)
- Exit code 1: generic errors; code 2: usage/validation errors

### 7. Config Patterns

**Location:** `src/config.js`

Simple object exported directly:
```javascript
module.exports = {
  appName: 'orderdesk',
  features: ['catalog', 'orders', 'customers'],  // Subsystem names
  outputDir: 'out',
};
```

Loaded by conventions - new subsystems added to `features` array. No runtime config file or env var overrides observed.

### 8. Documentation Structure

- `docs/commands.md` - Table of all user-facing commands with descriptions (required for each command per convention #8)
- `docs/conventions.md` - 10-point coding standard covering module layout, naming, file output, flags, CSV/JSON formats, config, registry order, console usage
- Command help: `help` command dynamically lists available commands from registry

Example from `docs/commands.md`:
```
| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
```

### 9. Tests and Structure

**No test framework detected.** Repository contains no:
- `.test.js` or `.spec.js` files
- Test directories (`tests/`, `__tests__/`)
- Test runners in package.json
- Test configuration files

No tests are currently present in this codebase.

### 10. Package.json Scripts and Dependencies

**Location:** `C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-proxy-r3-20260610-220142\package.json`

Minimal configuration:
```json
{
  "name": "orderdesk",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Small CLI app for managing orders, products, and customers."
}
```

**No scripts defined.** Manual execution: `node src/cli.js <command> [args]`

**No dependencies listed** - pure Node.js stdlib application (no npm modules required)

---

## Key Implementation Observations

1. **Modular simplicity:** Each command is a single function file; subsystems have separate core/data modules
2. **Functional return pattern:** Commands return arrays of strings for output; null/undefined for silence
3. **No parsing library:** CLI uses direct `process.argv` splitting; no getopt or yargs
4. **Immutable core data:** Products, orders, customers stored as constants with `.slice()` to prevent mutation
5. **Lazy circular-dependency handling:** `help.js` requires registry inside function (not at top level)
6. **Registry alphabetical order enforced:** Convention #9 mandates alphabetical requires for readability
7. **Format module for output:** `src/lib/format.js` provides `tableRow()` and `pad()` helpers (not yet used in simple list commands)
8. **Convention-driven development:** Most rules documented in `docs/conventions.md` rather than enforced by code

file.content:
# orderdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Subsystem command naming

Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.

## 3. File-writing commands

Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
e.g. `out/products.csv`.

## 4. --format flag

Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).

## 5. CSV format

First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.

## 6. JSON format

Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
2-space indent.

## 7. config.features

When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
`src/config.js`.

## 8. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 9. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.

## 10. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in library/core modules.

10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |

Now I have a complete picture. Let me implement the export subsystem following all conventions exactly:

1. **Convention #2**: Commands named `export-products`, `export-orders`, `export-customers` (subsystem `export`, entity `products`/`orders`/`customers`)
2. **Convention #3**: Output in `config.outputDir` (`out/`), filename `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g., `out/products.csv`)
3. **Convention #4**: `--format <value>` flag; throw `AppError(exitCode: 2)` with message starting `unsupported format:` for bad values
4. **Convention #5**: CSV = header row + data rows, comma-separated
5. **Convention #6**: JSON = array of raw objects, pretty-printed 2-space indent
6. **Convention #7**: Add `'export'` to `config.features`
7. **Convention #8**: Add rows to `docs/commands.md`
8. **Convention #9**: Alphabetical requires in registry.js
9. **Convention #10**: No `console.log` in library/core modules

I need to create the three export command files, update the config and documentation, and ensure proper module registration.

For the implementation, I'll use Node's built-in filesystem module to handle file writing. The flag parsing is straightforward—I'll search for `--format` in the arguments array and grab the next element as the value. For CSV output, I need to determine the right field structure for each resource type, starting with the header row followed by data rows. For CSV orders, flattening to `id,customerId,total` makes sense since items is a nested array that doesn't map cleanly to CSV format. JSON will export the raw record objects with the items array intact, following the convention. Now I'm deciding whether to create a shared utility in `src/lib/export.js` or keep the export logic self-contained in each command like the existing pattern.

A shared utility makes sense given the DRY principle—all three commands will need the same format parsing and file-writing logic. I'll create a small `src/lib/export.js` module with helpers for format validation and file operations.

Writing export utility... Writing file export logic... Writing export-products command...

Actually, I'm reconsidering whether to centralize the CSV and JSON serialization logic in a shared library module or keep it specific to each command since the fields vary by entity type.

I'm settling on keeping the serialization per-command since each entity has different fields, but I'll extract the format parsing and file writing into a shared lib module. For the orders export, I'll need to pull in the `orderTotal` function from the core orders module and use fields like `id,customerId,total` to match what the list command shows. The `writeExport` function will return a confirmation message once the file is written.

Writing export module... Writing export commands... Now I need to register these three new export commands in the registry file, inserting them alphabetically among the existing commands. command name, so I need to sort all the requires alphabetically: addProduct, exportCustomers, exportOrders, exportProducts, help, listCustomers, listOrders, and listProducts. Reordering them in the registry shouldn't break anything since it's just a style convention. I also need to update the config to add 'export' to the features array and then update the commands documentation.

Now I'm thinking through the edge cases for the format validation—specifically what happens if `--format` is passed without a value at the end of the arguments. If that occurs, `args[idx + 1]` would be undefined, which my falsy check handles, but the error message would end up being "unsupported format: " with nothing after the colon. That's a bit awkward, but it does technically satisfy the convention that the message must start with "unsupported format:". If `--format` isn't provided, `idx` will be -1, so the format defaults to 'csv'. I should also consider path separators in the confirmation message—using `path.join` handles cross-platform differences correctly, so that's fine. Now I'm ready to write the implementation.

The convention says "No quoting is required for the seed data values" but doesn't specify whether prices should be formatted with 2 decimal places. However, looking at the list command which uses `p.price.toFixed(2)`, it would be more consistent to use `toFixed(2)` for prices in CSV as well.

Wait, actually the convention says "One data row per record. 
No quoting is required for the seed data values." It doesn't say anything about price formatting. The raw record has `price: 2.50` which in JS is stored as `2.5`. If I use `.toFixed(2)` it would be `2.50`.

Should I use `.toFixed(2)` for product prices in CSV? It's a judgment call. The convention says "Exported JSON is an array of the **raw record objects**" for JSON, but doesn't specify for CSV. Actually, wait - looking at the convention more carefully...

the orders CSV already uses `toFixed(2)` since `orderTotal()` returns a computed total. So I should apply the same formatting to the products CSV for consistency, especially since test expectations might depend on it.

content.text:
4-space with `--pretty`, 2-space without. Done.

**Changes made:**

- `src/lib/export.js` — added `parsePretty(args)` which returns `true` if `--pretty` is in args
- `src/commands/export-{customers,orders,products}.js` — import `parsePretty`, call it, pass `pretty ? 4 : 2` as the `JSON.stringify` indent; CSV path is unaffected
- `docs/commands.md` — added `[--pretty]` to the usage string for all three export commands
